intro: Smoke Lab - BBQ is a community planning tool. Ideas below come from public brisket teaching (mainly Steve Gow / Smoke Trails BBQ). Not affiliated with or endorsed by the channel.

sources.title: 203°F Is DRYING OUT Your Brisket
sources.summary: Moisture loss, beef grades, and why pulling around 90.5 °C (195 °F) can stay juicier than riding to 203 °F on smoke alone.

sources.title: Brisket INTERNAL TEMP & Finishing Guide
sources.summary: Landmark probing around 82 °C (180 °F) and how collagen breakdown depends on both time and temperature.

sources.title: Do Smoker Mods Make a Difference? | Smoke Lab with Steve Gow
sources.summary: Fire baskets, scoops, stack extensions, and thermodynamics on offset cookers.

sources.title: Does Brisket STOP Absorbing Smoke at 140°F?
sources.summary: Smoke flavour and smoke ring can keep developing well past 60 °C (140 °F) on a wet bark.

sources.title: REVERSE SMOKED Brisket | The brisket wrap experiment
sources.summary: Wrap-first, smoke-later — lighter smoke profile, strong moisture retention.

sources.title: Steve Gow (Smoke Trails BBQ) — YouTube
sources.summary: Channel overview: pellet and electric rigs, brisket methods, and related cooks.

sources.title: The BEST offset fire management method for temperature control
sources.summary: Fire basket vs V-grate — steadier temps with less babysitting in the model comparison.

sources.title: The Easy Beginner Brisket Method That Works Every Time
sources.summary: Foil boat: top-down heat on the fat cap, bottom braises in juices.

sources.title: The JUICIEST BRISKET you'll ever eat: Brisket Confit
sources.summary: Smoke to ~74 °C, then ~18 hr in beef tallow at ~68.3 °C for a prime-rib-like texture.

sources.title: The Science of Brisket Tenderness (And Perfect Hold Time)
sources.summary: Primary reference for the exponential rendering model and temperature multipliers used in Smoke Lab.
